ground_truth.title: Top 27 Software Development Tools & Platforms [2025 List]
ground_truth.author: Mariusz Michalowski
ground_truth.main_content:
Building software today involves much more than just writing code. Developers rely on a range of specialized tools to manage complex workflows, collaborate seamlessly, and maintain high code quality and performance standards. From tools that handle real-time collaboration to those that automate testing, deployment, and monitoring, the right toolkit can make or break a project’s success. In this article, we will explore a selection of popular software development tools designed to tackle real-world challenges and elevate your development process.

What is a software development tool?
A software development tool is any specialized application or environment that assists developers in creating, testing, and maintaining software more effectively. These tools, ranging from code editors and debuggers to version control systems and deployment platforms, are designed to streamline specific stages of the development lifecycle. Software development tools often integrate directly into a developer’s workflow, enabling them to focus on building and improving software without getting bogged down by repetitive or error-prone manual processes.
Examples of software tools include Git, Jenkins, and Docker, each serving different purposes in software development and IT operations.

What are the main types of software development tools?
The main types of software development tools include Integrated Development Environments (IDEs), Version Control Systems (VCS), build automation tools, testing tools, and containerization tools.
The table below shows different types of software development tools:

Tool type	Description	Examples
Code editors	Lightweight editors for writing and editing code.	Visual Studio Code, Sublime Text, UltraEdit, Atom, Notepad++
Integrated Development Environments (IDEs)	IDEs combine a code editor, compiler, debugger, and other features to provide a comprehensive environment for coding.	JetBrains IDEs (IntelliJ IDEA, PyCharm), Eclipse IDE, NetBeans, Xcode
Version Control Systems (VCS) and code collaboration	Tools for tracking changes, managing repositories, and enabling team collaboration on codebases.	Git, GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, Perforce, Mercurial (See: GitLab vs GitHub)
AI coding assistants	AI assistants offer code suggestions, autocompletion, and aid in writing and debugging code.	GitHub Copilot, Codeium, Tabnine (See the full list: 17 Best AI-Powered Coding Assistant Tools)
Project management	Tools for organizing tasks, tracking progress, and managing team workflows.	Jira, Trello, Monday.com, Asana, ClickUp, Microsoft Project
Build automation	These tools automate the process of building and testing code, often used in continuous integration/continuous deployment (CI/CD) pipelines.	Jenkins, Gradle, Apache Maven, CircleCI, TeamCity, Spacelift
Containerization and orchestration tools	Packaging applications and dependencies for consistent deployment and scalability.	Docker, Kubernetes, OpenShift, Docker Swarm, Amazon ECS, Rancher
Development tools	Frameworks and tools for building applications and services.	Node.js, Bootstrap, React, Angular, Vue.js, Ruby on Rails
Database management	Tools to manage databases, perform queries, and visualize data structures and relationships.	MySQL Workbench, Microsoft SQL Server Management Studio, pgAdmin, Oracle SQL Developer, DBeaver
Testing and quality assurance	Automated testing for unit, integration, and API testing to ensure code quality.	Selenium, JUnit, Postman, Dynatrace
Cloud	Platforms for scalable computing, storage, and hosting in the cloud.	AWS, Azure, Google Cloud Platform, IBM Cloud, Oracle Cloud

Best software development tools
There are numerous options to consider when it comes to effective software development tools. Below, we’ll look at some of the best tools available to streamline your development process.
Top software development tools include:
1. Visual Studio Code
2. Sublime Text
3. UltraEdit
4. JetBrains IDEs
5. Eclipse IDE
6. Git
7. GitHub
8. GitLab
9. Bitbucket
10. GitHub Copilot
11. Codeium
12. Jira
13. Axure
14. Jenkins
15. Docker
16. Kubernetes
17. Node.js
18. Bootstrap
19. Firebase
20. Linx
21. SendBird
22. MySQL Workbench
23. Postman
24. Dynatrace
25. Amazon Web Services (AWS)
26. Azure
27. Spacelift

1. Visual Studio Code
Visual Studio Code (VS Code) is a free, open-source code editor developed by Microsoft, designed to provide a lightweight yet powerful development environment. It is available for Windows, macOS, and Linux. VS Code supports various programming languages and offers extensive customization through plugins, making it suitable for web development, data science, and more complex software projects.
Price: Free (Open-source)
Website: https://code.visualstudio.com/
Official documentation: https://code.visualstudio.com/docs

Key features
Intelligent code assistance: VS Code offers built-in IntelliSense, which provides intelligent code completion, parameter info, and quick info as developers type.
Integrated terminal and Git support: With an integrated terminal and Git commands, VS Code enables developers to manage repositories, commit changes, and push updates without switching applications.
Extensive plugin marketplace: VS Code has a vast library of extensions for language support, debugging, formatting, and themes, allowing developers to create a custom setup that aligns with their specific needs and preferences.

When to use Visual Studio Code
For front-end or full-stack development
When a lightweight, flexible editor is required
For quick setup and extensive extension options

Pros and cons
Pro: Lightweight yet powerful, enabling smooth performance even on less powerful systems
Pro: Highly customizable, allowing developers to create a tailored development environment with plugins and themes
Con: Some features, like specific debugging tools, require extensions that may impact performance when heavily used
Con: VS Code’s lightweight nature can make it feel less robust than full IDEs for managing larger or more complex enterprise-level applications

2. Sublime Text
Sublime Text is a fast, lightweight, and highly efficient text editor popular among developers for its responsiveness and minimalist design. Available for Windows, macOS, and Linux, Sublime Text is optimized for performance, making it ideal for handling large files and projects without lag. It is commonly used for web development, scripting, and general programming tasks.
Price: Paid license; offers an evaluation period
Website: https://www.sublimetext.com/
Official documentation: https://www.sublimetext.com/docs

Key features
Fast performance: Sublime Text is known for its quick response time and ability to handle large files with ease, enhancing productivity for developers working with extensive codebases.
Multi-caret editing: This feature allows users to edit multiple lines at once, making repetitive coding tasks faster and more efficient.
Plugin support via Package Control: Sublime Text offers an extensive selection of plugins through its Package Control, allowing users to add features, themes, and language support for a more tailored coding experience.

When to use Sublime Text
For fast performance on large files
For efficient multi-line or repetitive code editing
When a minimal, distraction-free editor is preferred

Pros and cons
Pro: Extremely lightweight and fast, even with large files, providing a smooth editing experience
Pro: Multi-caret editing and shortcuts streamline coding, making it efficient for repetitive tasks
Con: Some advanced features require a paid license, as Sublime Text is not fully free
Con: Limited built-in Git support, requiring plugins for full version control functionality

3. UltraEdit
UltraEdit is a versatile text editor designed for handling large files, complex data files, and code across multiple programming languages. It is widely used by developers, system administrators, and data analysts who require a powerful editor for text, code, and data manipulation tasks.
Price: Paid license; free trial available
Website: https://www.ultraedit.com/
Official documentation: https://wiki.ultraedit.com/Main_Page

Key features
Support for large files: UltraEdit can easily open and edit massive files (even multi-GB in size), making it ideal for working with data-heavy files or logs.
Multi-language syntax highlighting: UltraEdit provides syntax highlighting and code folding for various programming languages, enhancing readability and structure.
Advanced search and replace: With features like regex support, multi-line search, and search within files, UltraEdit offers powerful search capabilities for handling complex text manipulation.

When to use UltraEdit
For editing and managing large text or data files
For users needing advanced search, replace, and manipulation features
For multi-language coding in a powerful, customizable environment

Pros and cons
Pro: Optimized for large files and data manipulation, providing a smooth experience even with very large files
Pro: Highly customizable, allowing users to adjust the interface, themes, and settings to fit specific workflows
Con: Requires a paid license, which may not be ideal for users seeking free options
Con: The interface may feel complex for new users, especially those unfamiliar with advanced text editors

4. JetBrains IDEs
JetBrains IDEs are a suite of integrated development environments developed by JetBrains, tailored for specific programming languages and technologies. Known for their robust features, intelligent code assistance, and strong refactoring tools, JetBrains IDEs, including IntelliJ IDEA, PyCharm, WebStorm, and others, are popular choices among software developers seeking a powerful, language-specific development experience.
Price: Paid licenses; free trials, and free versions for certain products
Website: https://www.jetbrains.com/ides/
Official documentation: https://www.jetbrains.com/help/

Key features
Intelligent code assistance: JetBrains IDEs provide advanced code completion, real-time error detection, and refactoring tools to improve coding efficiency and accuracy.
Integrated debugging and testing tools: Each IDE includes built-in debugging and testing capabilities, allowing developers to run, test, and debug code within the environment.
Seamless VCS and deployment integration: JetBrains IDEs offer integrated support for version control systems like Git, making code management and deployment straightforward.

When to use JetBrains IDEs
For projects requiring strong language-specific support
Ideal for advanced debugging and refactoring
Useful when integrated version control is needed

Pros and cons
Pro: Comprehensive tools for language-specific development, optimizing workflows for languages like Java, Python, and JavaScript
Pro: Powerful debugging and refactoring features make development and troubleshooting more efficient
Con: Requires a paid license for full access to advanced features, which may be costly for some teams
Con: JetBrains IDEs can be resource-intensive, potentially slowing down performance on less powerful systems

5. Eclipse IDE
Eclipse IDE is an open-source integrated development environment widely used for Java development. It also supports other languages through plugins. Eclipse provides a rich set of tools and plugins, making it suitable for individual developers and large software development teams working on complex, multi-language projects.
Price: Free (Open-source)
Website: https://eclipseide.org/
Official documentation: https://help.eclipse.org/

Key features
Robust Java development tools: Eclipse offers powerful tools specifically for Java development, including code completion, debugging, and refactoring support.
Plugin ecosystem: With a wide range of plugins, Eclipse can be customized to support languages like Python, C++, and PHP, as well as frameworks and tools for DevOps and data science.
Project management and version control: Eclipse integrates with project management tools and version control systems (like Git), streamlining collaboration and workflow management.

When to use Eclipse IDE
For Java development and large-scale enterprise projects
For multi-language projects that require extensive plugin support
For teams needing an open-source, customizable IDE with integrated version control

Pros and cons
Pro: Highly customizable, allowing developers to create a tailored IDE environment with plugins and settings
Pro: Comprehensive Java support with extensive tools and libraries makes it a top choice for Java developers
Con: Can be resource-intensive, sometimes leading to slower performance on less powerful machines
Con: Due to its extensive feature set, the interface may feel complex and outdated, especially for new users

6. Git
Git is a free, open-source version control system created by Linus Torvalds, designed to track changes in code and coordinate work among software developers. With its speed, efficiency, and branching capabilities, Git is widely used in software development for both small projects and large collaborative codebases, enabling teams to work simultaneously without conflicts.
Price: Free (Open-source)
Website: https://git-scm.com/
Official documentation: https://git-scm.com/doc

Key features
Distributed version control: Each developer has a complete copy of the project history, allowing offline access and seamless collaboration.
Efficient branching and merging: Git supports lightweight branching and merging, enabling developers to create, test, and integrate new features without impacting the main codebase.
Commit history tracking: Git’s commit history provides a detailed log of code changes, allowing developers to track progress, revert to previous versions, and identify the origin of specific changes.

When to use Git
To manage and track changes in a codebase over time
For collaborating with multiple developers on the same project
When branching and testing new features independently from the main codebase

Pros and cons
Pro: Highly efficient for managing large projects and collaboration, as each developer can work independently and merge changes as needed
Pro: Git’s branching system encourages a flexible and streamlined workflow, making feature development and testing safer
Con: Git’s command-line interface can be complex for beginners, and there is a steep learning curve for those new to version control
Con: Merging conflicts can occur in collaborative settings, especially on large software development teams, requiring careful resolution

7. GitHub
GitHub is a web-based platform for version control and collaboration, built around the Git version control system. Owned by Microsoft, GitHub offers a wide range of tools for managing repositories, reviewing code, and coordinating projects, making it an essential resource for open-source projects and enterprise-level development alike. GitHub also serves as a social platform for software developers, enabling easy sharing and collaboration within the coding community.
Price: Free tier available; paid plans for additional features
Website: https://github.com/
Official documentation: https://docs.github.com/

Key features
Repository hosting and management: GitHub allows developers to host code repositories with built-in tools for version control, branching, and access management.
Pull requests and code review: The pull request feature enables developers to propose, review, and discuss code changes, fostering collaboration and improving code quality.
Integrated project management tools: GitHub offers project boards, issue tracking, and task assignment to help teams organize and manage development workflows effectively.

When to use GitHub
For hosting and sharing code with a global or local team
When managing projects with version control
For collaborative coding and code review

Use case example: How to Manage GitHub with Terraform

Pros and cons
Pro: Centralized platform for collaboration, making it easy for software development teams to contribute, review, and merge code
Pro: Extensive community and open-source resources, with a wide variety of publicly available projects and libraries
Con: The GitHub interface can be complex for beginners, especially when navigating branching, forking, and pull requests
Con: Private repositories and advanced features require a paid subscription for organizations and enterprise use

8. GitLab
GitLab is a comprehensive DevOps platform that combines source code management, CI/CD, and project management tools in one solution. With both cloud-hosted and self-managed options, GitLab enables teams to collaborate on code, automate deployments, and manage project workflows, making it suitable for organizations focused on a streamlined development-to-deployment pipeline.
Price: Free tier available; paid plans for enhanced features
Website: https://about.gitlab.com/
Official documentation: https://docs.gitlab.com/

Key features
Integrated CI/CD pipelines: GitLab’s built-in CI/CD tools allow teams to automate testing, build, and deployment processes directly within the platform.
Source code management and collaboration: GitLab provides tools for branching, pull requests, and code review, facilitating efficient and organized code collaboration.
Project management tools: GitLab includes features like issue tracking, milestone setting, and Kanban boards, helping teams manage project tasks and timelines in one place.

When to use GitLab
For teams seeking an all-in-one DevOps platform with integrated CI/CD
For organizations that prefer self-hosted solutions for data control
Useful when project management, code collaboration, and deployment need to be unified

Use case example: How to Manage Terraform State with GitLab
